narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.20, both close to what the model has seen in typical successful students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past student data, estimated your probability of failing at 49.4%—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your academic record that carried the most weight in the model's assessment. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.5 for students in the dataset; this suggested to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar exam struggles. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA played a helpful role, pulling the prediction toward success because your 3.20 is right around the typical 3.21 average, showing steady prior achievement. Similarly, your LSAT score of 36 helped your case by aligning closely with the dataset average of 36.7, indicating solid standardized test ability that the model views favorably for bar passage. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA were competitive with peers who typically pass, the lower Year 3 decile outweighed them, tipping the scales against you in this specific evaluation. It's worth noting that your full-time enrollment plans match the vast majority of students (over 92%), so that didn't factor heavily here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from an upper-middle-income family—many with similar backgrounds succeed—but rather a data-driven flag on that one academic area. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building stronger late-undergrad performance to boost your decile rankings, as schools often re-evaluate improved records. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or apply to programs with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories alongside metrics. With focused effort on those Year 3 weaknesses, your other strengths could shine brighter in the next round. Keep pushing; your profile shows real promise beneath this setback.
